talk_title: Therapy-associated clonal hematopoiesis and risk of secondary hematologic malignancies in cancer survivors

speaker.name: Ms. Berneo
speaker.affiliation: National Cancer Institute, Division of Cancer Epidemiology and Genetics

biomarkers.name: CHIP
biomarkers.finding: CHIP prevalence increased from 4.5% in non‑cancer to 7.5% in treated cancer survivors (≈50% relative increase)

biomarkers.name: MCA
biomarkers.finding: MCA prevalence rose from 3% in non‑cancer to 4% in solid tumor survivors (≈15% relative increase)

models.name: UK Biobank
models.genotype_or_key_features: 485,000 participants, 23,000 with prevalent solid tumor, ~4,000 with chemo/radiation exposure

clinical_data.population: cancer survivors with chemotherapy or radiation
cancer survivors with chemotherapy or radiation

clinical_data.metric: Hazard ratio for secondary hematologic malignancy with any MCA
Hazard ratio for secondary hematologic malignancy with CHIP

MOA_summary: Therapy (chemo/radiation) promotes expansion of clonal hematopoiesis, especially DNA‑damage‑response CHIP mutations and copy‑neutral loss‑of‑heterozygosity MCAs, which in turn increase the risk of secondary hematologic malignancies in cancer survivors.
key_findings: CHIP prevalence rises from 4.5% in cancer‑free individuals to 7.5% in treated cancer survivors (≈50% relative increase).
MCA prevalence rises from 3% to 4% in treated survivors (≈19% relative increase).
Therapy‑associated MCAs are distinct from those linked to secondary hematologic malignancy risk.
Presence of any autosomal MCA confers a hazard ratio of ~4.8 for secondary hematologic malignancy.
CHIP confers a hazard ratio of ~2.8 for secondary hematologic malignancy, with TP53 and PPM1D mutations linking therapy and malignancy risk.
open_questions: Will sex‑specific CH events further refine risk stratification?
How do ancestry and comorbidities modify therapy‑associated CH dynamics?

talk_title: Somatic APC mosaicism and colibactin‑producing bacteria in unexplained colorectal adenomatous polyposis

biomarkers.name: colibactin signature
biomarkers.finding: Detected in 40% of patients; associated with APC mutations in polyps

biomarkers.name: E. coli colibactin‑producing strain
biomarkers.finding: Detected in majority of polyps; concordant with genomic colibactin motif

models.name: Unexplained adenomatous polyposis cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 125 patients, 60 analyzed for APC mosaicism, 1/3 mosaic

clinical_data.metric: Median age at diagnosis (mosaic vs non‑mosaic)

MOA_summary: Somatic APC mosaicism arising early in embryogenesis drives polyposis in one‑third of unexplained cases, while colibactin‑producing E. coli induces a mutational signature that co‑occurs with APC mutations, together contributing to adenoma burden and earlier disease onset.
key_findings: APC mosaicism was identified in ~33% of tested unexplained polyposis patients.
Mosaic carriers presented at a younger median age (45 y) and had higher adenoma counts than non‑mosaic patients.
Colibactin‑associated mutational signatures were present in 40% of patients and often co‑occurred with APC mutations within the same lesion.
Blood detection of low‑frequency mosaic APC variants was possible in two cases, confirming systemic mosaicism.
Both mosaic APC and colibactin exposure represent distinct etiologic pathways for unexplained adenomatous polyposis.
open_questions: What is the relative contribution of bacterial colibactin versus APC mosaicism to cancer risk?
Can systemic screening for low‑frequency APC mosaicism improve early detection?

talk_title: Intrinsic and extrinsic determinants of racial disparities in breast cancer biology

biomarkers.name: COL1A1 collagen density
biomarkers.finding: Higher tumoral collagen in Black women tumors

biomarkers.name: CD4/CD8 T‑cell ratio
biomarkers.finding: Elevated CD4 and reduced CD8 in Black women; further increased with high poverty burden

models.name: VA HR‑positive breast tumor set
models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈60 archival tumors, oversampled Black women, annotated socioeconomic data

models.name: HCC1500, MDA‑175, MCF7, T47D
models.genotype_or_key_features: HR‑positive lines derived from Black or White patients

clinical_data.value: higher in Black women; further increased with high poverty
increased with collagen density for Black‑derived cells (p<0.05)
clinical_data.population: Black vs White HR‑positive cell lines

MOA_summary: Black women’s breast tumors exhibit higher collagen deposition and a CD4‑dominant, immunosuppressive T‑cell milieu; poverty amplifies the CD4 signature, and collagen‑rich environments enhance invasive potential of Black‑derived tumor cells, linking intrinsic ancestry‑related biology with extrinsic socioeconomic stressors.
key_findings: Tumors from Black women show significantly increased collagen content compared with White women.
CD4 T‑cell infiltration is enriched and CD8 depleted in Black women’s tumors; poverty burden further raises CD4 dominance.
Black‑derived HR‑positive cell lines require collagen‑containing matrix for 3D growth, unlike White‑derived lines.
HCC1500 xenografts (Black origin) form palpable tumors with radial collagen alignment; MDA‑175 (White) do not.
In silico invasion modeling predicts collagen density boosts invasion only for Black‑derived cells.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: White‑derived cell lines fail to thrive in collagen‑rich matrices, indicating selective microenvironmental dependence.
open_questions: Does targeting collagen remodeling improve outcomes specifically in Black patients?
Can socioeconomic interventions modify the tumor immune microenvironment?

talk_title: Microdysplastic cytomorphologic signatures in normal breast tissue predict future cancer risk

biomarkers.name: Epithelial microdysplastic signature
biomarkers.finding: Associated with ≥2‑fold increased breast cancer risk (HR≈2.1)

biomarkers.name: Stromal microdysplastic signature
biomarkers.finding: Associated with ≥2‑fold increased risk; strongest for ER‑negative cancers

models.name: Corman Tissue Bank
models.genotype_or_key_features: 3,415 healthy women donors, 104 later developed breast cancer, median follow‑up 6 y

models.name: Benign breast disease cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: Women with non‑proliferative disease, followed for cancer incidence

clinical_data.metric: Overall cancer risk with any microdysplastic signature
Risk for ER‑negative cancer with stromal signature
Risk in benign disease cohort with any signature

MOA_summary: Quantitative single‑cell analysis of H&E slides identifies epithelial nuclear enlargement and stromal inflammation signatures that reflect early oncogenic and immune alterations, conferring a two‑fold or greater future breast cancer risk independent of age and histologic atypia.
readouts: nuclear size, perimeter, chromasia, density metrics
key_findings: Four microdysplastic signatures (absent, epithelial, stromal, mixed) were defined based on nuclear and stromal metrics.
Presence of any signature conferred >2‑fold increased breast cancer risk after multivariable adjustment.
Stromal signature strongly predicted ER‑negative cancers, especially in stroma‑rich tissue.
Signatures were predictive across age groups (<50 and ≥50) and in benign disease cohorts.
RNA‑seq of signature‑positive tissues showed immune and wound‑repair pathways activation.
open_questions: Can integration of mammographic density improve predictive performance?
Will prospective application of the signatures guide personalized surveillance?

talk_title: Deep plasma proteomics for early‑stage breast cancer detection in dense breasts

biomarkers.name: Proteomic classifier score
biomarkers.finding: AUC 0.96 overall; 90.2% sensitivity in high‑density breasts at 90.4% specificity

models.name: Case‑control study
models.genotype_or_key_features: 335 women (116 early‑stage breast cancer, 219 healthy); 70% with density data

MOA_summary: A mass‑spectrometry‑based deep proteomic platform quantifies ~7,000 plasma proteins; a machine‑learning classifier distinguishes early‑stage breast cancer, achieving high sensitivity especially in women with dense breast tissue where conventional imaging is limited.
key_findings: Deep proteomics identified ~7,000 proteins per sample in both cancer and healthy cohorts.
Classifier achieved AUC 0.96, 86.2% sensitivity and 90.4% specificity overall.
In women with high breast density, sensitivity rose to 90.2% at fixed 90.4% specificity.
Stage‑0, I, II cancers detected with sensitivities of 84.6%, 88%, and 97% respectively.
Prospective pilot (n≈300) showed 89% sensitivity and 93% NPV, supporting clinical utility.

combination_strategies.rationale: Proteomic test can triage women who would benefit most from MRI
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved detection while reducing unnecessary MRIs

open_questions: Will assay performance be consistent across diverse racial/ethnic groups?
How will positive proteomic results impact downstream diagnostic work‑up and cost?

talk_title: Multi‑analyte liquid biopsy for early detection of pancreatic cancer in high‑risk individuals

biomarkers.name: 5‑hmC signature
biomarkers.finding: Distinguishes pancreatic cancer from non‑cancer with high specificity

biomarkers.name: cfDNA fragment size
biomarkers.finding: Shorter fragments enriched in cancer samples

biomarkers.name: Adjusted CA19‑9
biomarkers.finding: Improves tumor‑specific signal when combined with 5‑hmC and fragment size

MOA_summary: A multi‑analyte liquid biopsy combines 5‑hmC epigenomic profiling, cfDNA fragment‑size analysis, and genotype‑adjusted CA19‑9 to generate a machine‑learning classifier that discriminates pancreatic cancer from non‑cancer high‑risk individuals with >80% sensitivity and >97% specificity.
key_findings: Training set achieved 80.5% overall sensitivity and 97.5% specificity for pancreatic cancer detection.
Independent validation (n=1,445) confirmed 82.6% sensitivity and 97.5% specificity.
Performance remained consistent across three additional high‑risk validation cohorts.
Specificity loss across 11 non‑pancreatic cancers was negligible (<0.05%).
Pathway analysis of selected features recapitulated cancer hallmarks, confirming biological relevance.

combination_strategies.rationale: Combine blood‑based risk score with imaging to improve early detection
combination_strategies.phenotype: Higher PPV and earlier stage identification

open_questions: Can the assay detect pre‑diagnostic cancer in longitudinal samples?
What is performance in patients with IPMN or other premalignant pancreatic lesions?
